display_name: Gordon Lab — Gut Microbiome, Nutrition & Development

items.text: Is healthy postnatal growth in infants causally linked to the maturation of their gut microbial community, rather than merely correlated with it?
Which microbiome-directed complementary food (MDCF) ingredients most effectively repair an immature gut microbiota and restore healthy growth in undernourished children?
How do diet-induced shifts in gut microbial community composition influence host metabolic and immune signaling pathways that govern growth?
Gut microbiota immaturity — a community resembling that of a chronologically younger child — is a causal contributor to childhood undernutrition, not merely a consequence of it.
Specific complementary food ingredients can be rationally selected to promote the growth of beneficial, growth-associated bacterial taxa over disease-associated ones.

Gnotobiotic mice colonized with gut microbiota from discordant twin pairs transmit the donor's obese or lean phenotype, demonstrating a causal role for the microbiome in host metabolism (Ridaura et al., Science 2013).

A microbiome-directed complementary food (MDCF-2) outperformed standard ready-to-use supplementary food at repairing the gut microbiota and improving growth-associated plasma biomarkers in Bangladeshi children with moderate acute malnutrition (Chen et al., NEJM 2021).

Colonize gnotobiotic mice with fecal microbiota from a birth cohort of undernourished children, feed candidate MDCF prototypes, and quantify effects on growth biomarkers and repair of microbial community structure.
